title: Enterprise OData Design — API at Scale
description: Designing OData APIs that serve millions of requests. $expand N+1 problem analysis, deep entity operations, ETag concurrency control, batch operations, server-side paging strategies, OData security, and the decision framework for custom vs RAP-generated services.

description.value:
The decision between OData V2 and V4 is one of the most consequential API design choices for an SAP enterprise. OData V2 was introduced in 2009 and remains the most widely deployed OData version in SAP landscapes, largely due to its deep integration with SAP Gateway (IWFND/IWPRC). OData V4, standardized in 2014 and updated in 2020, introduces fundamental architectural improvements that address V2's scaling limitations.

Protocol Differences: OData V2 uses AtomPub (XML) as the default wire format with JSON (Verbose JSON) as an alternative. OData V4 mandates JSON as the default wire format and introduces a simplified JSON format that reduces payload size by 30-50%. V4 also introduces a formal metadata model (CSDL JSON) that is significantly more compact than V2's EDMX XML. For a service returning 10,000 entities, V4's JSON payload is approximately 40% smaller than V2's Atom format — a critical advantage at FAANG scale where bandwidth costs and latency are measured in milliseconds.

Performance Differences: V4 introduces server-side paging with @nextLink annotations as a core feature, whereas V2 required custom PageSize configuration and manual next link generation. V4's $apply query option enables server-side aggregation — equivalent to SQL GROUP BY — allowing the database to handle aggregation rather than transferring raw rows to the client. In benchmarks, V4 reduces roundtrips by 60% for aggregation-heavy workloads.

When to Upgrade: Upgrade to V4 when you need (1) server-side aggregation via $apply, (2) navigation property binding without $expand, (3) reduced payload size for mobile or low-bandwidth clients, (4) structured error handling via @ProtocolVersion headers, or (5) feature parity with cloud-native gateways (Azure API Management, Apigee) that have first-class V4 support. Stay on V2 when you have legacy SAP Gateway investments, third-party clients that only support V2, or integration with SAP PI/PO which has limited V4 support.

Staff+ Analysis: The V2-to-V4 migration is analogous to HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 migration — you must understand the protocol upgrade path, client compatibility matrix, and fallback strategy. FAANG companies handle this with versioned API gateways that can transparently transcode between protocol versions, allowing phased rollouts without breaking existing clients.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use OData V2 or V4 for this service?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the primary client type?" Fiori Elements / SAPUI5 = V4 is preferred (RAP-generated). Legacy mobile apps / existing SAP Gateway integrations = V2 for backward compatibility.

branches.condition: New Fiori Elements app with RAP backend
branches.path: OData V4 with RAP. Built-in $apply aggregation, reduced payload (JSON), nested $expand with $filter. V4 reduces roundtrips by 60% for aggregation-heavy workloads.

branches.condition: Existing SAP Gateway custom service with complex DPC/MPC
branches.path: OData V2. Migration to V4 would require full service rewrite. V2 is stable and well-supported. Upgrade when the service undergoes major redesign.
OData V4. JSON format reduces payload by 30-50% vs V2 Atom. Built-in delta tracking and server-side paging with @nextLink. Better for unreliable networks.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: SAP's direction is V4 with RAP. New development should be V4 by default. V2 is for maintenance of existing services. The payload savings alone (30-50%) justify V4 for high-throughput scenarios.

sections.title: The $expand N+1 Problem

description.value:
The $expand N+1 problem is the single most common performance issue in OData services at scale. When a client issues a request like /SalesOrderSet?$expand=Items, the naive implementation executes one query for the header and then one additional query per header to fetch items. If the result set contains 1000 sales orders, that's 1 + 1000 = 1001 database roundtrips — catastrophic at enterprise scale.

Root Cause: The N+1 problem occurs when the OData service implementation treats each entity as an isolated query unit. In ABAP, this happens when the GET_ENTITYSET method fetches headers and then calls GET_EXPANDED_ENTITY or GET_ENTITY for each header's child entities. Each call triggers a separate SELECT statement, a separate database cursor, and a separate network roundtrip between the application server and the database.

Mitigation Strategies: The primary mitigation is eager loading — fetch header and items in a single query using a JOIN or batch fetch. In ABAP, this means using a single SELECT with JOIN or using FOR ALL ENTRIES with a pre-fetched key set. A secondary mitigation is query batching — collect all required keys and issue a single, batched query for all child entities. A tertiary mitigation is result caching — cache the expanded results with TTL-based or invalidation-based expiry.

FAANG Context: The $expand N+1 problem is identical to GraphQL's N+1 problem and Facebook's DataLoader pattern. The standard FAANG solution is a batching resolver — collect all parent IDs, batch-load all children in one query, return them grouped by parent ID. This is exactly the same as ABAP's FOR ALL ENTRIES pattern. At Staff+ level, you should propose a caching layer (like DataLoader) that automatically batches expanded queries regardless of the order they arrive.

y="113">GET SalesOrder</text><rect class="sys-box" x="440" y="180" width="160" height="60"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="520" y="200">FOR ALL ENTRIES</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="520" y="220">GET Items (batched)</text><path d="M600,108 L660,108" class="hi-edge"/><path d="M600,210 L660,210" class="ok" stroke="#2ecc71" stroke-width="2" fill="none"/><text class="sublbl" x="520" y="310" class="ok">2 queries total</text></svg>

" Optimized $expand using FOR ALL ENTRIES
" Instead of querying items per header (N+1),
" batch load all items in 2 queries.

METHOD get_expanded_salesorders.
  " 1. Fetch headers (1 query)
  SELECT so_id, customer, total_amount
    FROM salesorder
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_headers)
    WHERE so_id IN @iv_range.

  " 2. Collect all header keys
  DATA lt_so_ids TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF so_id.
  lt_so_ids = VALUE #( FOR header IN lt_headers ( header-so_id ) ).

  " 3. Fetch ALL items with single query
  "    (2 queries total, regardless of header count)
  SELECT so_id, item_id, product, quantity, price
    FROM salesorderitem
    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_items)
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_so_ids
    WHERE so_id = @lt_so_ids-table_line.

  " 4. Group items by parent header
  DATA ls_result LIKE LINE OF et_result.
  LOOP AT lt_headers INTO DATA(ls_header).
    CLEAR ls_result.
    ls_result-header = CORRESPONDING #( ls_header ).
    LOOP AT lt_all_items INTO DATA(ls_item)
         WHERE so_id = ls_header-so_id.
      APPEND ls_item TO ls_result-items.
    ENDLOOP.
    APPEND ls_result TO et_result.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

sections.codeBlock:
" Optimized $expand using FOR ALL ENTRIES
" Collect all keys, batch-load items in 2 queries total
METHOD get_expanded_salesorders.
  SELECT so_id, customer, total_amount
    FROM salesorder INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_headers)
    WHERE so_id IN @iv_range.
  DATA lt_so_ids TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF so_id.
  lt_so_ids = VALUE #( FOR header IN lt_headers ( header-so_id ) ).
  SELECT so_id, item_id, product, quantity, price
    FROM salesorderitem INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_all_items)
    FOR ALL ENTRIES IN @lt_so_ids
    WHERE so_id = @lt_so_ids-table_line.
  LOOP AT lt_headers INTO DATA(ls_header).
    INSERT VALUE #(
      header = CORRESPONDING #( ls_header )
      items  = VALUE #( FOR item IN lt_all_items
                 WHERE ( so_id = ls_header-so_id )
                 ( CORRESPONDING #( item ) ) )
    ) INTO TABLE et_result.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

sections.title: Deep Entity Operations

description.value:
Deep entity operations allow a single OData request to create, update, or delete a parent entity along with its related child entities in one atomic operation. This is critical for enterprise scenarios where a Sales Order must be created with all its line items, delivery details, and pricing conditions as a single transactional unit.

In OData V2, deep insert is achieved by including inline navigation properties in the POST payload. The SAP Gateway framework processes these as nested create operations within a single logical unit of work (LUW). However, the default behavior in many implementations applies the parent create first, and if a child fails, the parent is NOT automatically rolled back — the developer must handle this explicitly.

In OData V4 and RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model), deep operations are a first-class concept. The RAP framework automatically wraps deep operations in a single transactional LUW. If any child entity fails validation or database insert, the entire operation rolls back. This is a significant reliability improvement over older custom implementations.

Transactional Integrity: For deep operations at enterprise scale, you must consider: (1) ordering of operations — parent before child for creates, child before parent for deletes; (2) error propagation — a child failure must abort the entire operation with a meaningful error response; (3) partial success — some protocols return HTTP 200 with embedded errors per entity; (4) idempotency — the same deep create should not create duplicate entities if retried. The Staff+ engineer designs for all four.

" Deep Insert: SalesOrder with Items (RAP-based)
" RAP automatically wraps this in a single LUW

CLASS /dmo/cl_salesorder_action IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD create_salesorder_deep.
    " RAP handles deep operations via BO structure
    " The request body contains header + items in one payload
    
    DATA: ls_salesorder TYPE STRUCTURE FOR CREATE /dmo/r_salesorder.
    
    " Header data
    ls_salesorder = VALUE #(
      %cid = 'Header'
      customer_id = '1000001'
      order_date = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( )
      total_amount = '1500.00'
      currency_code = 'USD'
      
      " Deep items (inline navigation)
      %control = VALUE #(
        customer_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        order_date = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        total_amount = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        currency_code = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      )
    ).
    
    " Item detail - part of the same deep payload
    ls_salesorder = VALUE #( BASE ls_salesorder
      items = VALUE #(
        ( %cid = 'Item1'
          product_id = 'PROD-001'
          quantity = '10'
          price = '100.00'
          %control = VALUE #(
            product_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
            quantity = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
            price = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
          )
        )
        ( %cid = 'Item2'
          product_id = 'PROD-002'
          quantity = '5'
          price = '100.00'
          %control = VALUE #(
            product_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
            quantity = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
            price = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
          )
        )
      )
    ).
    
    " Single MODIFY triggers deep insert
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_salesorder
      ENTITY salesorder
        CREATE FROM ls_salesorder
      FAILED DATA(ls_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(ls_reported).
    
    IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
      " Entire operation rolled back automatically
      " Return structured error to client
    ENDIF.
    
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" Deep insert: SalesOrder with Items (RAP)
" RAP wraps this in a single LUW — atomic commit or rollback
DATA ls_salesorder TYPE STRUCTURE FOR CREATE /dmo/r_salesorder.
ls_salesorder = VALUE #(
  %cid = 'Header'
  customer_id = '1000001'
  order_date = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( )
  total_amount = '1500.00'
  currency_code = 'USD'
  %control = VALUE #(
    customer_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
    order_date  = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
    total_amount = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
    currency_code = if_abap_behv=>mk-on )
  items = VALUE #(
    ( %cid = 'Item1'
      product_id = 'PROD-001' quantity = '10' price = '100.00'
      %control = VALUE #(
        product_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        quantity   = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        price      = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) )
    ( %cid = 'Item2'
      product_id = 'PROD-002' quantity = '5' price = '100.00'
      %control = VALUE #(
        product_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        quantity   = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
        price      = if_abap_behv=>mk-on ) ) ) ).
MODIFY ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_salesorder
  ENTITY salesorder CREATE FROM ls_salesorder
  FAILED DATA(ls_failed)
  REPORTED DATA(ls_reported).
IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
  " Entire operation rolled back
  " Return structured error to client
ENDIF.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Deep Entity Operations — N+1 Query Cost vs Batch Cost

variables.name: Parent entities per request
variables.source: Sales order batch sync from e-commerce

deep_with_expand.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Optimized: 1 query fetches all headers, 1 query fetches all items (FOR ALL ENTRIES), 1 query fetches all schedules. ~150ms total. 3 round-trips regardless of depth.

n_plus_one.monthlyPenalty: $6,000 — Naive: 1 for headers + 50 for items + 500 for schedules = 551 round-trips × 2ms = 1.1s network alone + query execution = ~6s total. At 100 concurrent requests, this saturates the database connection pool.

calculation.breakeven: Optimized deep expand is 40x faster at 3 levels of depth. Even at 1 level (parent + children), the optimized approach (2 queries) is 25x faster than N+1 (1+50=51 queries). Always implement batch loading for $expand.

description.value:
ETag (Entity Tag) concurrency control is the standard mechanism for optimistic locking in RESTful APIs and OData services. An ETag is an opaque identifier representing the version of an entity. When a client sends an update request with the If-Match header containing the ETag, the server checks if the current version matches. If the entity has been modified by another transaction, the ETag won't match and the server returns HTTP 412 (Precondition Failed).

In OData, ETags are typically implemented using a timestamp-based or hash-based mechanism. The timestamp approach stores a last_changed_at field on each entity — the ETag is the UTC timestamp formatted as a string. The hash approach computes a cryptographic hash of the entity's key fields and returns it as the ETag. The timestamp approach is simpler but introduces a race condition if two writes occur within the same millisecond; the hash approach is more robust but adds computational cost on every read.

Implementation in ABAP: In SAP Gateway, ETag handling requires: (1) setting the last_modified_timestamp field in the model; (2) implementing IF_BSP_SERVICE~GET_LAST_MODIFIED in the BSP application; or (3) using the %is_dirty flag in RAP. The SAP Gateway framework automatically generates ETags from the last_changed_at field if it's defined in the entity type. On update, the framework checks the If-Match header against the current value and returns 412 on mismatch.

Staff+ Design: At enterprise scale, ETag collision handling requires a systematic strategy. For a service handling 10,000 updates per second, ETag retries must be expected and planned for. Implement exponential backoff and jitter in the client retry logic. Consider whether strong ETags (byte-exact representation) or weak ETags (semantic equivalence, prefixed with W/) are appropriate. For SAP business objects, weak ETags are often preferred because the same logical state may have different physical representations (e.g., different formatting of amounts).

" ETag-based optimistic locking in RAP

CLASS /dmo/cl_salesorder_action IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD update_salesorder.
    " The framework receives If-Match header automatically
    " and compares with the current ETag value
    
    MODIFY ENTITIES OF /dmo/r_salesorder
      ENTITY salesorder
        UPDATE FROM VALUE #(
          (
            %cid_ref = 'HeaderRef'
            so_id = '1000001'  " Key field
            customer_id = '1000002'
            " %if_etag is automatically checked by framework
            %control = VALUE #(
              customer_id = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
            )
          )
        )
      FAILED DATA(ls_failed)
      REPORTED DATA(ls_reported).
    
    IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
      " Check for ETag mismatch
      LOOP AT ls_failed-salesorder INTO DATA(ls_fail).
        IF ls_fail-%fail-cause = if_abap_behv=>cause-conflict.
          " HTTP 412 - Precondition Failed
          " Client must re-fetch and retry
          APPEND VALUE #(
            msgty = 'E'
            msgv1 = 'Entity modified by another user'
            msgv2 = 'Retry with fresh ETag'
          ) TO et_messages.
        ENDIF.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
  
  " Custom ETag generation (hash-based)
  METHOD get_etag_hash.
    DATA: lv_hash TYPE string.
    
    CONCATENATE:
      ls_entity-so_id,
      ls_entity-last_changed_at,
      ls_entity-total_amount
    INTO DATA(lv_raw).
    
    " SHA-256 hash as strong ETag
    cl_abap_hmac=>calculate_hmac(
      EXPORTING
        if_algorithm = 'SHA256'
        if_key       = 'secret-salt'
        if_data      = lv_raw
      IMPORTING
        ef_hmacb64   = lv_hash
    ).
    
    rv_etag = |"{ lv_hash }"|.  " RFC 7232 format
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I use ETags for concurrency control in this OData service?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Can two users edit the same entity simultaneously?" If yes (concurrent editing expected), implement ETags. If entities are created once and rarely updated, ETags add overhead with no benefit.

branches.condition: Multiple concurrent editors, financial or critical data
branches.path: Strong ETag (hash-based). Compute SHA-256 hash of key fields + last_changed_at. Server compares If-Match on every update. Returns HTTP 412 on conflict. Client must re-fetch and retry.
Weak ETag (timestamp-based). Use last_changed_at field as ETag. Simpler but race-condition risk within same millisecond. Prefix with W/ for semantic equivalence per RFC 7232.
No ETags. Skip ETag handling entirely. Saves the computational cost of hash generation on every read and the round-trip for If-Match check on writes.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: ETags implement optimistic locking — detect conflicts at write time rather than preventing them at read time. Use strong ETags for financial data, weak ETags for master data, and no ETags for append-only logs.

sections.title: Batch Operations

description.value:
OData batch operations allow multiple CRUD operations to be grouped into a single HTTP request, reducing network roundtrips and improving throughput. A batch request is a multipart MIME message containing multiple change sets and query operations. Each change set is a group of create, update, or delete operations that are executed as a single transactional unit — either all succeed or all fail.

In SAP Gateway, batch requests are processed by the /sap/opu/odata/iwbep/GW*/$batch endpoint. The framework parses the multipart request, executes each operation or change set in order, and returns a multipart response with individual status codes per operation. The atomicity of change sets is guaranteed within the ABAP LUW — if any operation in a change set fails, all prior operations in that change set are rolled back.

At enterprise scale, batch operations are essential for (1) mobile clients with unreliable connectivity — send one batch instead of 50 individual requests; (2) bulk data loading — insert 500 line items in a single batch; (3) transactional workflows — update Sales Order header and add two new items atomically; (4) dashboard applications — fetch 10 different entity sets in one request.

Performance Considerations: Batch size must be balanced against server memory and processing time. SAP recommends a maximum of 100 operations per batch. Larger batches increase server memory pressure because the entire batch payload is buffered before processing. For batches exceeding 5 MB, consider chunked uploads or streaming. The batch processing time must also respect the ABAP server's timeout limits — typically 300 seconds for RFC, but HTTP timeout may be shorter depending on the gateway configuration.

sections.codeBlock:
" OData batch request example (multipart MIME)
" Changeset: update header + create item atomically
" Content-Type: multipart/mixed;boundary=batch_123

"--batch_123
"Content-Type: application/http
"Content-Transfer-Encoding: binary
"
"PATCH /sap/opu/odata/SAP/Z_SALESORDER_SRV/SalesOrderSet('1001')
"Content-Type: application/json
"If-Match: W/"2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
"
"{"TotalAmount": "2500.00"}
"
"--batch_123
"Content-Type: application/http
"Content-Transfer-Encoding: binary
"
"POST /sap/opu/odata/SAP/Z_SALESORDER_SRV/SalesOrderSet('1001')/Items
"Content-Type: application/json
"
"{"Product": "PROD-003", "Quantity": 3, "Price": "500.00"}
"
"--batch_123--

" ABAP server-side processing
" Framework changeset: all or nothing within a changeset
" Multiple changesets = multiple independent LUWs

quantifiedTradeoff.title: OData Batch — Throughput Improvement vs Complexity Cost

variables.name: Operations per batch
variables.source: Mobile app sync of 50 sales orders

individual_requests.monthlyPenalty: $9,000 — 50 sequential requests: each adds 50ms network + 150ms processing = 200ms × 50 = 10,000ms. Mobile connection may not survive 10s of continuous requests.

batched_request.monthlyPenalty: $1,200 — Single batch with 50 operations: 1 round-trip (50ms) + 50 × 150ms serialized = 7,550ms. 24% improvement over individual. But main benefit is reliability — 1 request vs 50.

parallel_batch.monthlyPenalty: $0 — SAP Gateway processes change sets sequentially within a changeset but can parallelize independent change sets. 5 changesets × 10 ops = 5 parallel tracks × 150ms = ~160ms total. 62x faster than individual.

calculation.breakeven: Batch always wins for >3 operations. The breakeven is at 3 operations: 3 × 200ms = 600ms individual vs 3 × 150ms + 50ms = 500ms batched. At 50 operations, batch is 62x faster with parallel change sets.

sections.title: Server-Side Paging & Security

description.value:
Server-side paging is the mechanism by which an OData service partitions large result sets into manageable pages, returning a continuation token (@nextLink in OData V4, __next in V2) that the client uses to fetch the next page. Without server-side paging, a single request returning 1 million records would consume gigabytes of memory on both server and client, and the serialization/deserialization time would cause HTTP timeouts.

Continuation Tokens: The design of the continuation token is critical for enterprise scalability. A well-designed continuation token is opaque to the client and encodes: (1) the last processed key value (or set of keys), (2) the page size, (3) the filter context, and (4) a timestamp or sequence number for consistency. The token should be URL-safe, base64-encoded, and optionally encrypted to prevent tampering. The server must validate the token on each paginated request — stale or invalid tokens should return HTTP 400.

CSRF Protection: OData services are vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks. SAP Gateway implements CSRF protection via the X-CSRF-Token header pattern: the client first sends a GET request with X-CSRF-Token: Fetch, the server responds with a token (typically a UUID), and the client includes this token in all modifying requests (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE). At enterprise scale, CSRF token generation and validation must be efficient — token generation is typically O(1) using a server-side session store or stateless JWT-like signed tokens.

Staff+ Design for Paging: When designing server-side paging at scale, consider: Keyset pagination (cursor-based) vs offset pagination (skip/top). Offset pagination degrades as offset increases (scanning and discarding rows). Keyset pagination uses a WHERE clause on the last seen key — O(1) per page regardless of position. For OData, implement keyset pagination using $filter with gt (greater than) on the key field, combined with $top. This is the approach used by all FAANG APIs (Twitter, Stripe, GitHub). Avoid $skip for result sets exceeding 10,000 records.

sections.codeBlock:
" Server-side paging with keyset cursor
data lv_cursor type string.

METHOD get_entityset.
  DATA: lt_result TYPE TABLE OF zsalesorder,
        lv_last_so_id TYPE so_id.

  " Decode cursor (base64-encoded last key)
  IF iv_cursor IS NOT INITIAL.
    lv_last_so_id = decode_cursor( iv_cursor ).
  ENDIF.

  " Keyset pagination: WHERE on last seen key
  SELECT * FROM zsalesorder
    INTO TABLE @lt_result UP TO @iv_pagesize ROWS
    WHERE so_id > @lv_last_so_id
    ORDER BY so_id ASCENDING.

  " Build next cursor from last record
  IF lines( lt_result ) = iv_pagesize.
    DATA(ls_last) = lt_result[ lines( lt_result ) ].
    DATA(lv_next) = encode_cursor( ls_last-so_id ).
    set_response_header( name = '@nextLink'
      value = |/SalesOrderSet?$top={ iv_pagesize }&cursor={ lv_next }| ).
  ENDIF.

  et_entityset = CORRESPONDING #( lt_result ).
ENDMETHOD.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I secure this OData service?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Who are the consumers?" Internal Fiori users = ABAP authorization (PFCG) + CSRF protection. External APIs = OAuth 2.0 with scopes. Public APIs = API key + rate limiting + OAuth 2.0.

branches.path: SAP Gateway authorization (PFCG roles + AUTH object). CSRF token via X-CSRF-Token: Fetch pattern. Row-level security via CDS DCL. No HTTPS termination needed if within SAP network.

branches.condition: External API consumed by partner or mobile app
branches.path: OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant. Scopes per entity type (read, write). API key for rate limiting (1000 req/h per key). HTTPS with TLS 1.2+. Whitelist allowed IP ranges.

branches.condition: Public API (open to internet, no authenticated users)
branches.path: API key authentication + rate limiting (100 req/min per key). IP whitelist. Request size limit (1MB max). Sensitive data fields must be excluded via CDS projection. WAF (Web Application Firewall) recommended.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Security in OData follows defense-in-depth: network (HTTPS + IP whitelist), identity (OAuth 2.0 / PFCG), data (DCL row-level security), and transport (CSRF tokens). Never rely on a single security layer.

sections.title: Custom vs RAP-Generated Decision

description.value:
One of the most important architectural decisions for a Staff+ SAP engineer is choosing between custom OData services (built with SAP Gateway IWFND/IWPRC, or direct ABAP on REST) and RAP-generated services (built with ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model). This decision has profound implications for development velocity, maintenance cost, performance, and extensibility.

RAP-Generated Services are the recommended approach for new development. RAP provides: auto-generation of CRUD operations from CDS views, built-in transactional processing, automatic ETag support, deep entity operations, draft handling, and integration with SAP Fiori elements. RAP-generated services follow SAP's Clean Core strategy — they extend without modifying standard SAP objects. The tradeoff is that RAP is opinionated: you work within its framework constraints, and highly customized behavior requires significant workarounds.

Custom Services are appropriate when: (1) the data model is too complex for RAP's declarative approach (e.g., multi-source federation with different backends); (2) the service requires non-standard protocol behavior (e.g., custom headers, non-standard error formats); (3) existing SAP Gateway investments with extensive custom code in GET_ENTITYSET and GET_ENTITY methods that would be too costly to migrate; (4) the service must aggregate data from both SAP and non-SAP systems and the transformation logic is complex.

Staff+ Decision Framework: The Staff+ engineer uses a weighted decision matrix across six dimensions: (1) Development Velocity — RAP is 3-5x faster for standard CRUD, custom is faster for complex behavior; (2) Maintenance Cost — RAP reduces long-term cost through framework upgrades, custom requires manual maintenance; (3) Performance — RAP's generated SQL is optimized for CDS views, custom can be fine-tuned for specific query patterns; (4) Flexibility — custom offers unlimited flexibility, RAP is constrained by framework capabilities; (5) Security — RAP provides built-in authorization via CDS access control, custom requires manual authorization checks; (6) Extensibility — RAP services are inherently extensible via key-user and developer extensibility patterns, custom services require explicit extension points. A service scoring above 70% on flexibility and complexity should be custom; otherwise, use RAP.

sections.codeBlock:
" Decision: RAP for standard CRUD, custom for complex federation
"
" RAP-generated:
" @OData.publish: true on CDS view generates full CRUD
" Pros: Auto ETag, deep entity, draft handling
" Cons: Limited to single CDS data source
"
" Custom service (SAP Gateway IWPRC):
" METHOD get_entityset REDEFINITION.
"   " Multi-source federation
"   SELECT FROM zorders INNER JOIN zext_system
"     ON zorders~source_id = zext_system~system_id
"     INTO TABLE @et_entityset.
"   " Custom headers
"   io_response->set_header( name = 'X-Custom-RateLimit'
"     value = '1000' ).
" ENDMETHOD.
"
" Guideline: Start with RAP, extend only when RAP limits hit

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our deep insert for sales orders is unreliable. Sometimes the header is created but the items fail, and we end up with orphan headers."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Wrap the deep insert in a BAPI instead of using OData." (workaround, doesn't fix the OData service)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Orphan headers on deep insert failure mean the operation is not atomic. In OData V2 custom services, the parent and child creates are separate operations — if a child fails after the parent commits, there's no automatic rollback. The fix depends on your stack: (1) If using RAP (OData V4), the framework automatically wraps deep operations in a single LUW — if any child fails, the entire operation rolls back. Confirm your BDEF uses managed scenario. (2) If using custom SAP Gateway (V2), you must implement manual transaction handling: execute all creates in a temporary buffer, validate everything, then commit with COMMIT WORK. If any step fails, call ROLLBACK WORK and return a meaningful error. Use a 'savepoint' pattern — write header with status 'PENDING', write items, then update header to 'COMPLETE'. A cleanup job removes any records stuck in 'PENDING' for >1 hour. (3) Add idempotency keys to the request — if the client retries after a timeout, the service should detect the duplicate and return the existing result instead of creating a duplicate header."
adversarialResponse.pattern: atomicity-restoration — identify the root cause (atomicity gap in non-RAP OData), provide stack-specific solutions (RAP LUW vs custom transaction handling), and add defense mechanisms (savepoints, cleanup jobs, idempotency keys).

details: Staff+ Insight: API design is a core FAANG Staff+ skill. The same principles apply whether you're designing OData for SAP or REST/gRPC for a FAANG microservice. Frame OData discussions in FAANG API terms: resource modeling, pagination, versioning, rate limiting, idempotency, and error contracts.
